frame_analyses.description: The first instruments enter the field and the needle is grasped and oriented near the upper portion of the Penrose drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Brief early regrasping is needed, but the needle is ultimately loaded in a usable forehand orientation.

frame_analyses.description: The needle is advanced toward the marked slit and the first bite is driven through the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Instrument approach is controlled, though the setup is somewhat deliberate rather than fluid.

frame_analyses.description: Both suture limbs are now visible and the stitch has been placed across the slit at the marked region.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The drain remains fixed with no avulsion; apparent bite placement is close to target with only small deviation.

frame_analyses.description: A double-wrap first throw is being formed before the knot is seated down onto the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Wrap count appears consistent with a surgeon's knot; right-hand lead is the best estimate from instrument motion.

frame_analyses.description: The second throw is constructed with alternating hand position and the knot stack is being squared.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: There is a noticeable pause for regrasping, but tension is kept controlled and the knot does not loosen.

frame_analyses.description: The trainee transitions into the final alternating throw and begins to flatten the knot stack.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Hand switch appears to be preserved, though the exact lead hand is partly obscured by crossing instruments.

frame_analyses.description: The third throw is tightened and the final knot sits compactly against the drain.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: The knot appears secure with acceptable apposition and no obvious slip.

frame_analyses.description: A blue cutting instrument is introduced and the suture tails are isolated for trimming inside the trainer.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Cutting is careful but somewhat prolonged, adding time late in the task.

frame_analyses.description: Both suture ends have been cut and only short tails remain at the completed knot.
frame_analyses.technique_notes: Final construct remains intact, with the drain still attached and the slit well approximated at the stitch.

knot_assessments.notes: Best estimate is a right-led double throw; wrap pattern appears consistent with a surgeon's knot, though partially obscured during looping.
Alternation appears preserved on the second throw. The throw looks like a single wrap, with some extra time spent regrasping before seating.
Final throw appears to alternate back to the right lead and seats the knot stack flat without obvious loosening.

technique_summary: Overall this was a controlled, successful run with accurate stitch placement, no drain avulsion, and a final knot that appeared secure after trimming. The main inefficiency was the amount of time spent regrasping and organizing the suture between later throws and before cutting, which lengthened the total time more than any apparent technical error. Task was not fast but was not chaotic; strongest in control and construct quality.
improvement_suggestions: Reduce regrasping between throws by organizing the two limbs earlier and committing to the next lead hand sooner after each knot is seated.
Load the needle more decisively at the start so the first drive can begin with less early repositioning.
Bring the cutting instrument in sooner and expose both tails cleanly before trimming to shorten the final phase.
strengths: The stitch appears to pass close to the intended marked region with only small apparent deviation.
The drain stayed firmly attached throughout with no avulsion and good tissue handling.
The final knot stack remained intact after cutting and the slit looked well approximated at the stitch site.

rubric_assessment.overall_judgment: Clean, successful, mid-efficiency Task 5 performance. Held back far more by time consumption than by technical penalties. Highest confidence in no-avulsion / no-gap / secure-final-knot conclusions; lower confidence in exact mm deviation estimate and precise lead-hand assignment on final throw.
biggest_time_losses: Suture cut phase at 28.3s — prolonged tail isolation before trimming
Third throw at 28.5s with crossing-instrument regrasping
Second throw at 26.0s with pause before seating
rubric_assessment.uncertainty_notes: Moderate confidence (0.68). Highest confidence in no-avulsion / no-gap / secure-final-knot conclusions. Lower confidence in exact mm deviation and precise lead-hand assignment on final throw due to oblique camera angle and crossing instruments.
